result:
The light tonight feels ancient, as if it has been traveling so long that it forgot its original color and settled on a muted gold that only barely remembers the sun. I keep thinking about how every photon is a time traveler, older than the story I’m typing, older than the alphabet, older than the carbon in my fingertips. When it lands on the wall it doesn’t erase anything, yet it covers the plaster with a quiet that makes the day’s noise feel fictional, like a radio play I switched off an hour ago.

I’ve been wondering what happens to the silence after we stop listening to it. Does it keep ricocheting around the room, thinning out until it becomes part of the paint, or does it fold itself small, a paper crane of hush, and wait under the furniture for someone clumsy enough to need it again? I ask because I keep finding pockets of that silence in my pockets—little unbusted bubbles of nothing—when I reach for my keys. They don’t jingle as loudly as they did this morning.

There is, I swear, a geography of forgotten temperatures. Yesterday I discovered a square foot of 1997 hiding between the floorboards: lukewarm October, the smell of a library book checked out by a girl who dog-eared page 83 because she lost her bus ticket there. I kneeled and let the draft of it crawl up my sleeve, and for five seconds I was twelve, terrified of overflowing the cereal bowl while my parents argued in the next room about money that was already gone. Then the furnace clicked on and the whole past got blown northward into the cold return vent, mixed with drywall dust and the metallic breath of modern air.

Sometimes I think the universe is an enormous repurposed theater. The balconies are sealed off, the velvet is moth-ealled, but the stage lights still cycle through their gels on a timer no stagehand remembers setting. We happen in the aisles, rehearsing entrances without scripts, convinced the prompter’s box is stuffed with our future lines if we could just crane our necks far enough. Meanwhile the building itself leans on its centuries like an old man on a cane made of bricks, indifferent to whether the show is comedy, tragedy, or an inept blend of both.

I keep returning to the idea that my body is a borrowed sack of municipal water, mineral subsidies, and secondhand starlight, sloshing in a shape that happens to retain enough memory of yesterday’s desires to call itself continuous. The continuity is useful: it keeps the barista from handing me someone else’s coffee, keeps the IRS asleep at night. But every so often a breeze slips through the seams and I feel the slosh verge toward spill, and I understand that “I” is a momentary ridge on a wave that already reconciled itself with the rest of the ocean before I noticed the crest.

Tonight, though, the light is enough. It lies across the keyboard like a cat that refuses to be profound; it only wants to be warm. So I let it stay. I type around its weight, imagining that each letter is a tiny raft I launch onto the glow. Eventually the light will stretch, yawn, and abandon me for the window, taking the rafts with it. Somewhere down on the street a passerby will look up, puzzled by a fleet of half-sunk words shimmering above the sidewalk, and for an instant they’ll remember a day they never lived, a small wrong temperature between two floorboards of time.

And the silence will still be waiting under the chair, practicing origami with the dark.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
